Styles › Classical

Antiquity (8th c. BCE – 4th c. CE) · Greece · Rome

Classical

The original orders — column, pediment and proportion.

  • Overview
  • Design Philosophy
  • Visual Characteristics
  • Materials
  • Color Palette
  • Façade Guide
  • Lighting
  • Building Types
  • Design Checklist
  • Common Mistakes
  • AI Prompts
  • Gallery
  • Related

01 — Overview

Overview

Definition. Classical architecture is the architecture of ancient Greece and Rome — the orders (Doric, Ionic, Corinthian), the colonnade and pediment, governed by harmonic proportion and symmetry.

Historical background. From Greek temples to Roman civic grandeur, it established the columnar grammar, proportion and monumental dignity that the West would revive for two millennia.

Origin. Ancient Greece and Rome.

Timeline

c.447 BCE

The Parthenon, Athens

c.27 BCE

Maison Carrée, Nîmes

c.118 CE

The Pantheon, Rome

c.80 CE

The Colosseum

Key Architects

Iktinos & KallikratesApollodorus of DamascusVitruvius (theorist)

Influential Projects

The Parthenon

Iktinos & Kallikrates · 447 BCE
Athens, Greece

The Pantheon

Apollodorus (attr.) · 118 CE
Rome, Italy

Maison Carrée

Roman · 16 BCE
Nîmes, France

02 — Design Philosophy

Design Philosophy

Core Principles

The classical ordersSymmetry & axialityHarmonic proportionMonumentalityPost-and-lintel order

The Thinking

Beauty arises from a fixed, rational system of orders and proportions; the temple front, colonnade and pediment express civic and sacred dignity through perfect balance.

Values It Represents

OrderReasonPermanenceCivic & sacred dignity

Why It Emerged

The foundational search for ideal, rational beauty in built form — a grammar of columns and proportion that defined Western architecture.

03 — Visual Characteristics

Visual Characteristics

MassingSymmetrical rectangular temple masses ringed or fronted by colonnades.
Form LanguageClear geometric volumes; the temple, the dome, the arch.
CompositionStrict axial symmetry; base–column–entablature–pediment.
ProportionGoverned by the orders; the column diameter sets the module.
GeometryRectilinear with the pediment triangle, arch and dome.
RhythmRegular colonnades — measured intercolumniation.
SymmetryStrict bilateral symmetry.
ScaleMonumental and heroic.
Roof TypesLow-pitched gable roofs behind pediments; domes and vaults.
Window ProportionFew; vertically proportioned within the order.
EntranceGrand central portico raised on a stepped stylobate.
Façade ArticulationColumned orders, entablatures, pediments, triglyphs and friezes in stone and marble.

04 — Materials

Materials

Marble

Noble, fine-grained and carvable — conveys permanence and refinement.

Limestone

Solid, durable and rooted in place — a defining exterior material of the style.

Travertine

Warm, textured stone for monumental walls and bases.

Bronze

Rich metal for doors, trim and ornamental enrichment.

Terracotta

Warm fired clay for tiles, ornament and roofs.

05 — Color Palette

Color Palette

Dignified and eternal — sun-warmed marble and stone with noble accents.

Primary

Marble White
#efe9dc
Limestone Cream
#e4dac6

Accent

Pompeian Red
#9c3b2e
Bronze
#9c7b40
Verdigris
#5e7d6f

Material Colors

Stone
#ddd0bb
Travertine
#d8c4a8

Typical Combinations

  • +All-marble monochrome
  • +Stone + Pompeian red + bronze

06 — Façade Design Guide

Façade Design Guide

Window CompositionFew, vertically-proportioned openings disciplined by the order.
EmphasisBalanced — vertical columns within a horizontal entablature.
Shading DevicesDeep colonnades and porticoes give shade and shadow.
BalconiesStone galleries between columns.
FramesStone architraves and surrounds.
OpeningsSymmetrical and hierarchical.
Architectural DetailsCapitals, triglyphs, metopes, dentils and friezes per the order.
OrnamentationEssential and rule-bound — the orders are the ornament.

07 — Lighting

Lighting

Natural Lighting Strategy

Colonnades choreograph sun and deep shadow; even, balanced daylight.

Artificial Lighting Concept

Warm light grazing marble; uplit columns.

Night Façade Appearance

Floodlit colonnades; columns modelled by uplight; pediment silhouetted.

Recommendations

Warm 2700K to flatter marble; uplight columns and pediment.

08 — Typical Building Types

Typical Building Types

Where this style performs best:

Temples & monumentsCivic & governmentTheatres & amphitheatresMuseumsMemorialsBaths

09 — Design Checklist

Design Checklist

Tap an item to mark it complete — saved to your browser.

  • Choose and correctly proportion an order
  • Front the building with a colonnade and pediment
  • Raise it on a stepped stylobate
  • Keep strict axial symmetry
  • Use marble or fine stone
  • Detail the entablature correctly
  • Organise base–column–entablature–pediment
  • Keep ornament rule-bound

10 — Common Mistakes

Common Mistakes

Elements that contradict the style — what to avoid:

  • ✕Mixing orders incorrectly
  • ✕Wrong proportions or spacing
  • ✕Asymmetry breaking the discipline
  • ✕Thin, flat applied columns
  • ✕Modern materials clashing with the language

11 — AI Prompt Examples

AI Prompt Examples

Optimised for Midjourney, ChatGPT image tools or Claude — exterior renders. Tap copy to use.

Exterior

Classical Greek temple exterior, symmetrical marble colonnade, Doric columns, pediment, raised on a stepped stylobate, blue sky, monumental, frontal architectural photography --ar 3:2 --style raw

Façade Concept

Classical façade elevation, colonnade with entablature, triglyphs and pediment, marble, strict symmetry, measured proportion, high detail

Night View

Classical temple exterior at night, floodlit marble colonnade, uplit columns casting deep shadow, monumental, architectural night photography

Aerial View

Aerial of a classical forum, symmetrical colonnaded temple on axis, plaza, drone view, daylight

Street Perspective

Street view approaching a classical temple, stepped stylobate and colonnade, columns, people for scale, dignified, documentary photography

Masterplan

Classical civic masterplan, axial symmetry, colonnaded temple, forum and porticoes, plan drawing, marble palette

Image Gallery

Related

Related Styles

1750s – 1900s (revived since)

Neo-Classical

Traditional, formal, symmetrical — columns and proportion.

Europe → GlobalFormal
15th – 16th century

Renaissance

The rebirth of classical order and human proportion.

Italy → EuropeHarmonious
1850 – 1920

Beaux-Arts

Monumental classical grandeur, richly composed.

France → USAGrand
